description: Email copy evaluation rubric. 10 dimensions, weighted. Adapted from the meta-ad rubric for email-specific evaluation.

dimensions.name: Angle Clarity
dimensions.description: Is the strategic angle unmistakable from the subject + first paragraph?

scoring_guide.1: No clear angle. Subject and body could be about anything. Reader deletes or skims.
scoring_guide.2: Angle is vaguely present but buried below the fold or muddled with multiple themes.
scoring_guide.3: Angle is present but competes with other themes. Reader needs effort to identify the core message.
scoring_guide.4: Angle is clear and dominant from subject through body. Minor distractions exist.
scoring_guide.5: Angle is unmistakable. Subject, preheader, and body all reinforce a single strategic position.

dimensions.name: Motivation Match
dimensions.description: Does the email hit the intended buying motivation — the felt need, not just the stated need?

scoring_guide.1: Motivation is absent or wrong. The email speaks to a need the audience does not feel.
scoring_guide.2: Motivation is present but superficial. Names the category without tapping the emotional driver.
scoring_guide.3: Motivation is addressed but at a rational level only. The felt need is implied, not activated.
scoring_guide.4: Motivation is clearly present and emotionally resonant. The reader sees themselves.
scoring_guide.5: Motivation is deeply felt. The email articulates what the audience feels but hasn't put into words.

dimensions.name: Tactic Execution
dimensions.description: Is the email structure (hook → story/proof → CTA) properly executed?

scoring_guide.1: No recognisable structure. Email is a list of features or announcements with no narrative logic.
scoring_guide.2: Structure attempted but breaks down. Opening doesn't connect to body, or body doesn't lead to CTA.
scoring_guide.3: Structure present and mostly follows the framework, but one transition is weak or forced.
scoring_guide.4: Well-executed. Opening, body, and CTA form a coherent arc with smooth transitions.
scoring_guide.5: Flawlessly executed. Each section builds on the last. Structure is invisible — it just reads naturally.

dimensions.name: Subject Line Power
dimensions.description: Would this subject line get opened? Evaluates curiosity, specificity, and relevance.

scoring_guide.1: Generic or spammy subject. 'Update from us' or 'Don't miss out!' No reason to open.
scoring_guide.2: Mildly relevant but bland. Could be any brand. Reader skips unless they're already engaged.
scoring_guide.3: Relevant and somewhat engaging. Would get opened by warm leads but not cold.
scoring_guide.4: Strong subject that creates curiosity or recognition. Most target readers would open.
scoring_guide.5: Exceptional subject. Specific, personal, and impossible to skip. Creates a knowledge gap that demands opening.

deterministic_rules.personalization: Uses reader context (name, location, prior action) = bonus
deterministic_rules.specificity: Contains number, name, or concrete detail = bonus
deterministic_rules.spam_triggers: 'Free', 'Act now', 'Limited', ALL CAPS, excessive punctuation = penalty

dimensions.name: Body Flow & Readability
dimensions.description: Is the email scannable, well-paced, and easy to read on mobile?

scoring_guide.1: Wall of text. No line breaks, no formatting. Reader abandons after 2 sentences.
scoring_guide.2: Some structure but dense paragraphs. Hard to scan on mobile.
scoring_guide.3: Reasonable structure. Short paragraphs but could be tighter. Some filler.
scoring_guide.4: Well-paced. Short paragraphs, clear rhythm. Easy to scan. Minor tightening possible.
scoring_guide.5: Perfect flow. Every line earns its place. Scannable, mobile-friendly, no filler. Reads in under 60 seconds.

dimensions.name: CTA Clarity
dimensions.description: Is there one clear, compelling call-to-action? Is it obvious what happens when they click?

scoring_guide.1: No CTA, or multiple competing CTAs. Reader doesn't know what to do.
scoring_guide.2: CTA exists but vague or buried at the bottom after reader has lost interest.
scoring_guide.3: CTA is clear but generic ('Click here', 'Learn more'). Not compelling.
scoring_guide.4: CTA is clear, compelling, and well-positioned. Reader knows what happens next.
scoring_guide.5: CTA is irresistible. Perfectly placed, action-specific, and creates urgency without being pushy.

dimensions.name: Personalization & Relevance
dimensions.description: Does the email feel personal and relevant to the reader's context?

scoring_guide.1: Mass blast. No personalization. Reads like it was sent to 100,000 people.
scoring_guide.2: Minimal personalization (first name only). Content is generic.
scoring_guide.3: Some relevance to the reader's situation. References their context (waitlist, interest area).
scoring_guide.4: Feels personal. References specific actions, timing, or context. Reader feels seen.
scoring_guide.5: Deeply personal. Reads like a 1:1 message from someone who knows their situation.

dimensions.name: Specificity
dimensions.description: Concrete numbers, names, and details — not vague claims.

scoring_guide.1: Entirely vague. No numbers, no concrete details.
scoring_guide.2: One concrete detail present but surrounded by vague claims.
scoring_guide.3: Multiple concrete details present. Some claims remain vague.
scoring_guide.4: Consistently specific throughout. Numbers are verifiable.
scoring_guide.5: Every claim is concrete and fact-checked. Specific costs, counts, timeframes.

dimensions.name: Opening Hook
dimensions.description: Does the first line of the email body (after opening) create enough pull to keep reading?

scoring_guide.1: Generic opener. 'Hi [name], just wanted to reach out...' No reason to continue.
scoring_guide.2: Mildly interesting but predictable. Reader skims ahead.
scoring_guide.3: Relevant and somewhat engaging. Would keep warm leads reading.
scoring_guide.4: Strong opener that creates curiosity or recognition. Most readers continue.
scoring_guide.5: Exceptional hook. Immediately personal, specific, and pulls the reader in.

dimensions.name: Differentiation
dimensions.description: Is this email distinct from others in the sequence? Does it add new value?

scoring_guide.1: Duplicate of another email. Same message, same structure, same CTA.
scoring_guide.2: Surface-level differences only (different subject, same argument).
scoring_guide.3: Different hook or angle but core message overlaps with another email in sequence.
scoring_guide.4: Clearly distinct. Reader seeing both emails would recognize different messages.
scoring_guide.5: Completely unique perspective within the sequence. Different emotional entry point, different proof.

dimensions.name: Opening Diversity
dimensions.description: Does this email open differently from other emails in the set? Measures first-word and first-three-word uniqueness.

scoring_guide.1: First 3 words identical to another same-type email. Feels like a copy-paste opening.
scoring_guide.2: First word shared with 3+ other same-type emails. The set sounds monotone at the start.
scoring_guide.3: First word shared with 2 other same-type emails. Some opening variety but room to diversify.
scoring_guide.4: First word shared with only 1 other same-type email. Good opening variety.
scoring_guide.5: First 3 words are unique across all same-type emails. Every email opens with a distinct voice.

dimensions.name: Sentence Variance
dimensions.description: Does the email body have rhythmic variety — a mix of short punchy sentences and longer narrative ones?

scoring_guide.1: Monotone — all sentences are the same length (std dev <= 1). Reads like a list.
scoring_guide.2: Minimal variety (std dev > 1). Slight length differences but still flat rhythm.
scoring_guide.3: Moderate variety (std dev > 3). Some short and long sentences but could be more dynamic.
scoring_guide.4: Good rhythm (std dev > 5). Mix of punchy and expansive sentences creates flow.
scoring_guide.5: Great rhythm variety (std dev > 8). Copy has a natural, engaging cadence.

dimensions.name: Emotional Register
dimensions.description: Does this email use a different emotional opening style from the rest of the set?

scoring_guide.1: Over 50% of same-type emails share this register. Locked into one opening style.
scoring_guide.2: 30-50% of same-type emails share this register. Limited diversity.
scoring_guide.3: 15-30% of same-type emails share this register. Moderate diversity.
scoring_guide.4: Less than 15% of same-type emails share this register. Good variety.
scoring_guide.5: Unique register — no other same-type email opens this way.
